questions.question: Define the standard error of the mean (SEM). Explain why it decreases as the sample size increases.
questions.modelAnswer: The SEM is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean. It is calculated as σ/√n, where σ is the population standard deviation and n is the sample size. As n increases, the denominator √n increases, causing the overall value of the SEM to decrease. A larger sample size leads to a more precise estimate of the population mean, hence less variability from sample to sample.

questions.question: A dataset of network latency times has a population standard deviation (σ) of 20 ms. You calculate the mean latency from a random sample of 100 packets. What is the standard error of the mean for your sample? Show your calculation.
questions.modelAnswer: The formula for the standard error of the mean is SEM = σ / √n. σ = 20 ms, n = 100. Therefore, SEM = 20 / √100 = 20 / 10 = 2 ms.

questions.question: Explain the fundamental difference between standard deviation and standard error. Discuss how each measure is used in the context of statistical inference and why confusing them can lead to significant misinterpretation of data.
questions.modelAnswer: Standard deviation describes the variability or spread of the raw data within a single sample or population. Standard error describes the variability or precision of a sample statistic (like the mean) across multiple hypothetical samples from the same population. Standard deviation is used to understand the distribution of the data itself. Standard error is used in inferential statistics to construct confidence intervals and conduct hypothesis tests about population parameters. Confusing them is critical because it can lead to overestimating or underestimating the precision of estimates, resulting in incorrect conclusions about the reliability of study findings.
